sentences.base_language: Tonight there is a wedding and I need to get ready.
sentences.target_language: الليلة هناك حفل زفاف وأحتاج إلى الاستعداد.
sentences.romanized: al-layla hunak hafl zifaf wa-ahtaj ila al-isti'dad.
sentences.literal: Tonight there wedding-party and-I-need to the-preparation.
sentences.grammarNote: هُنَاك (hunāk, there is) introduces a new topic existentially — similar to French 'il y a.' حَفل زِفَاف (ḥafl zifāf, wedding party) — iḍāfa: حَفل (party/gathering, from root ح-ف-ل, to celebrate) + زِفَاف (wedding, from ز-ف-ف, to lead a bride). أَحتَاج إِلى (aḥtāj ilā, I need) — Form VIII of ح-و-ج (to need): always takes إِلى, never a direct object alone. الاِستِعدَاد (al-istiʿdād, the preparation) — Form X verbal noun of ع-د-د.

sentences.base_language: First I take a warm shower.
sentences.grammarNote: أَستَحِمّ (astaḥimm, I shower/bathe) — Form X of ح-م-م (ḥ-m-m, to be hot/bathe): اِستَحَمَّ = to take a bath/shower. The doubled م (shaddah) in the root causes the final ـمَّ. بِمَاء دَافِئ (bi-māʾ dāfiʾ, with warm water) — بِـ + indefinite noun + adjective. دَافِئ (dāfiʾ, warm) — active participle فَاعِل from د-ف-أ (d-f-ʾ, to be warm).

sentences.base_language: Then I dry my hair with a towel.
sentences.grammarNote: ثُمَّ (thumma) = then / after that. Arabic has several words for sequencing: أَوَّلاً (firstly), ثُمَّ (then), بَعدَ ذلك (after that), أَخيراً (finally). ثُمَّ always connects two separate actions and is more formal than the conjunction وَ (wa = and).

sentences.base_language: I put on a white shirt and dark trousers.
sentences.grammarNote: قَمِيصاً أَبيَض (qamīṣan abyaḍ, a white shirt) — both words are in the indefinite accusative (tanwin ـاً) as the direct object and its adjective. أَبيَض (abyaḍ, white) follows the colour pattern أَفعَل — colour adjectives in Arabic use this special form. بَنطَالاً دَاكِناً (banṭālan dākiyan, dark trousers) — same pattern: indefinite accusative noun + adjective. بَنطَال is a loanword from Italian/French.

sentences.base_language: Then I fix my hair in the mirror.
sentences.grammarNote: أُرَتِّب (urattib, I arrange/tidy) — Form II of ر-ت-ب (r-t-b, to arrange): رَتَّبَ = to put in order (intensive/repeated action). شَعرِي (shaʿrī, my hair) — شَعر (hair, collective noun) + suffix ي (my). أَمَام المِرآة (amāma al-mirʾāh, in front of the mirror) — أَمَام (in front of) is a positional preposition. المِرآة (the mirror) — from root ر-أ-ي (to see): literally 'the seeing-device.'

sentences.base_language: I put on some cologne and check that I look neat.
sentences.target_language: أضع بعض العطر وأتأكد من أنني مرتب.
sentences.romanized: ada' ba'd al-'itr wa-ata'akkad min annani murattab.
sentences.literal: I-put some the-perfume and-I-make-sure from that-I neat.
sentences.grammarNote: أَتَأَكَّدُ مِن أَنَّ (ata'akkad min anna) = I make sure that. مِن أَنَّ (min anna) introduces a that-clause after verbs of checking, knowing, or being sure. The pronoun suffix on أَنَّ agrees with the subject: أَنَّني (that I), أَنَّكَ (that you), أَنَّهُ (that he).

sentences.base_language: My friend Sami knocks on the door.
sentences.grammarNote: صَدِيقِي سَامِي (ṣadīqī sāmī, my friend Sami) — possessive noun صَدِيقِي (my friend) + the proper name سَامِي as an appositive. يَطرُق الباب (yaṭruq al-bāb, he knocks on the door) — يَطرُق from root ط-ر-ق (ṭ-r-q, to knock/strike): طَرَقَ الباب = to knock on the door; also طريق (road) from the same root — a path that has been beaten/struck.

sentences.base_language: I open the door and he comes in.
sentences.grammarNote: وَهُو يَدخُل (wa-huwa yadkhul, and he enters/comes in) — the independent pronoun هُو (he) is used for emphasis before the verb: it makes the subject explicit. In Arabic, the subject is already encoded in the verb, so adding هُو is emphatic or contrastive. أَفتَح (aftaḥ, I open) — Form I of ف-ت-ح (f-t-ḥ, to open): فَتَحَ, verbal noun فَتح.

sentences.base_language: He looks at me and smiles.
sentences.grammarNote: يَنظُر إِلَيَّ (yanẓur ilayya, he looks at me) — يَنظُر from root ن-ظ-ر (n-ẓ-r, to look): نَظَرَ إِلى = to look at. إِلَيَّ (ilayya, at me) — إِلى + first-person suffix: the ى becomes ـيَّ before the suffix ي. يَبتَسِم (yabtasim, he smiles) — Form VIII of ب-س-م (b-s-m, to smile): اِبتَسَمَ = to smile (inceptive Form VIII).

sentences.base_language: You look great! Are you ready?
sentences.grammarNote: تَبدُو رَائِعاً (tabdū rāʾiʿan, you look wonderful) — تَبدُو from root ب-د-و (b-d-w, to appear): Form I, defective verb. رَائِعاً (rāʾiʿan, wonderful — accusative) is the predicate of تَبدُو (a linking verb). مُستَعِدّ (mustaʿidd, ready) — Form X active participle of ع-د-د (ʿ-d-d, to count/prepare): اِستَعَدَّ = to get ready. Both هُو and أَنتَ forms work for the predicate.

لِنَذهَب! (li-nadhhab) = Let's go! This is the cohortative (hortatory) form: لِـ + first-person plural verb. It expresses a suggestion or command to oneself and others: لِنَأكُل (let's eat), لِنَبدَأ (let's begin), لِنَتَحَدَّث (let's talk).
